content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 26, 1986

Rich Milner
Office of Assemblyman Mike Roos
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: FPPC No. A-86-203

Dear Mr. Milner:

This is in reply to your memorandum dated June 17, 1986, to
Jay Greenwood concerning the application of the mass mailing
prohibition contained in Gov. Code Section 89001.

QUESTION

You have asked for clarification, on behalf of the Assembly
Rules Committee, of how to handle mass mailings to individuals
specifically signed up to be periodically informed on specific
legislative issues.

ANSWER

Gov. Code Section 89001 prohibits mass mailings sent at public
expense by or on behalf of an elected state officer after the
elected state officer has filed a declaration of candidacy for any
office.

FPPC Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18901 interprets the
terms used in Gov. Code Section 89001. With regard to your
question concerning communications to "individuals specifically
signed up to be periodically informed on specific legislative
issues," Section 18901(b) provides:

A mailing is a mass mailing if 200 or more identical or nearly
identical pieces of mail are sent in a calendar month,
provided, however, that mass mailings do not include mailings
which contain only information or material sent in response to
specific requests contained in written correspondence,
petitions, oral inquiries or coupons.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Alden Milner
June 26, 1986
Page 2


Based on this provision, mailings sent to individuals who
request information on specific legislative issues are not
prohibited under Gov. Code Section 89001. As we discussed
earlier, it is not necessary to retain the written document
requesting the information. However, we suggest that a record of
some sort, such as an indication on the mailing list, be
maintained to indicate that a person has requested information,
with a notation as to the specific subject matter. With regard to
what constitutes a "specific request" for information, the request
must indicate that the person is requesting information on a
specific topic or subject matter. Therefore, a request for "all
legislative information," for example, would not fit within the
exception provided in Section 18901(b).

I hope this provides adequate guidance to the Rules Committee
with regard to mailings to persons who have requested information.

Please call me if you have any additional questions.

Sincerely,

Jeanne Pritchard
Division Chief, Technical
Assistance & Analysis Division

SACRAMENTO ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814
(916) 445-7644

DISTRICT OFFICE ADDRESS
600-SO. NEW HAMPSHIRE AVE.
LOS ANGELES CA 90005
(213) 386-8042

Assembly
California Legislature

COMMITTEES:
HUMAN SERVICES
NATURAL RESOURCES
POLICY RESEARCH
PUBLIC INVESTMENT, FINANCE AND
INTERGOVERNMENTAL
WAYS AND MEANS
JOINT RULES COMMITTEE
COMMITTEE ON GOVERNOR'S
RESIDENCE AND EXECUTIVE OFFICE
SPECIAL SUBCOMMITTEE ON
STATE ADMINISTRATION

JUN 13 8 41 AM '86

MIKE ROOS
MAJORITY FLOOR LEADER
ASSEMBLYMAN, FORTY-SIXTH DISTRICT

June 17, 1986

MEMORANDUM

TO:  JAY

FROM:  RICH

RE:  ADVISORY COMMITTEES

Rules Committee would like to get something in writing to
advise them on how to handle mass mailings for advisory
committees -- individuals specifically signed up to be
periodically informed on specific legislative issues.

As you will recall, we discussed this in May.  At your
convenience, I would appreciate some informal, but in-writing
guidance to them.

Thanks.
